en.extract:
The band-tailed nighthawk (Nyctiprogne leucopyga) is a species of nightjar in the family Caprimulgidae. It is widely spread throughout northern and central South America. Five subspecies are recognised. They are nocturnal birds that feed on flying insects. As with almost all nightjars, they lay their eggs directly on the ground and rely their camouflaged plumage to avoid predation.


Taxonomy
The band-tailed nighthawk was formally described and illustrated in 1825 by the German naturalist Johann Baptist von Spix under the binomial name Caprimulgus leucopygus. Spix specified the habitat as the wooded shores of the Amazon. The specific epithet is derived from Ancient Greek λευκοπυγος/leukopugos meaning "with white rear", from λευκος/leukos meaning "white" and -πυγος/-pugos meaning "-rumped". The band-tailed nighthawk is now placed together with the plain-tailed nighthawk in the genus Nyctiprogne that was introduced in 1847 by the French naturalist Charles Lucien Bonaparte.


Subspecies
There are 5 recognized subspecies which differ in size, colour and vocalizations. The subspecies N. l. latifascia has been suggested to be a separate species. It is visually distinct from the other subspecies, as its plumage is darker in colour, and there are differences in its vocalizations.

N. l. exigua Friedmann, 1945 – east Colombia and south Venezuela.  Smaller and has darker that the nominate.
N. l. pallida Phelps, WH & Phelps, WH Jr, 1952 – northeast Colombia and west, central Venezuela. Paler upperparts than the nominate.
N. l. leucopyga (Spix, 1825) – east Venezuela, the Guianas and north Brazil
N. l. latifascia Friedmann, 1945 – extreme south Venezuela
N. l. majuscula Pinto & Camargo, 1952 – northeast Peru, north, east Bolivia and west, central Brazil. Largest of the subspecies, paler with white underparts.


Description
The band-tailed nighthawks is a small species, with a body length of 16.5 to 19 cm (6.5 to 7.5 in), and a wingspan of 12.9 to 14.1 cm (5.1 to 5.6 in). Adults weigh from 0.023 to 0.028 kg (0.051 to 0.062 lb). The female has a larger average weight and wingspan than the male.
The bill is very wide but short, with a length of 0.9 to 0.97 cm (0.35 to 0.38 in), and is hooked at the tip. It has cryptically patterned brown plumage, used to blend in with tree bark and leaves. Its wings are long with pointed wingtips, and have no white markings. It has a black bill, dark brown irises, and pinkish-gray tarsus and feet. The tarsus lengths are generally short, from 1.18 to 1.24 cm (0.46 to 0.49 in). Unlike many nightjar species, band-tailed nighthawks lack elongated rictal bristles. It has white patches on either side of the throat, and a white band on the mid-tail, across its three outermost tail feathers. The tail itself is short, and somewhat forked, at 8.2 to 9.7 cm (3.2 to 3.8 in).
It has very weak sexual dimorphism, with both sexes presenting similar plumage and colouring. The immature and juvenile phases are also similar to the mature adult, although are slightly more plain and paler.


Distribution and habitat
The band-tailed nighthawk has a large range, although there are gaps in its distribution. It is located through much of the northern parts of South America, from east of the Andes to Venezuela and central Brazil. It is found from northern Venezuela and eastern Colombia, east to French Guiana and eastern Brazil. Its range then extends south to northern Bolivia and northeastern Paraguay. It is also found in Peru, Ecuador, and Guyana.
Its natural habitat is in subtropical and tropical areas located near water, along rivers, streams, marshes, and lakes. These habitats include lowland forests, savannahs, grasslands, and swamps. It is only reported in areas of elevation below 200 to 300m.


Behaviour and ecology
The band-tailed nighthawk is a nocturnal species, and is the most active during the late evening, early morning, and at night. During the day, it roosts near the ground in locations with dense thickets of bushes and trees. The ecology and behaviour of this nighthawk is not well known, and there have been few dedicated studies


Breeding
Seasonal changes in the vocal activity of the band-tailed nighthawk has been used to determine the timing of its breeding season. The breeding season begins between July and August, when the maximum singing activity occurs, and the dry season ends. The nesting period occurs between September and October, when the wet season begins and there is a high amount of food availability. There is also less vocal activity measured during this nesting period.
The nest is a shallow hole on the ground, usually in open areas with little coverage, such as gravel beaches or forest floors. The nest is lined with leaves and other vegetation. The eggs are cream or white, with small dark blotches.


Feeding
The band-tailed nighthawk is an insectivore that feeds primarily on aerial arthropods, using a feeding method known as hawking. Its diet includes beetles, ants, true bugs, and flying termites, amongst other arthropods. It forages in continuous flight, with rapid, shallow wingbeats and gliding to keep it aloft. It feeds mostly at dusk, when there are high amounts of flying insects.
Feeding occurs around sunset and stops once it becomes too dark for the bird to see its prey, and then occurs once more just before dawn. In between feeding sessions, it roosts in the same area, on thin horizontal branches close to vegetation and water. The band-tailed nighthawk roosts perpendicular to the branch, either by itself or in groups of 2-10 individuals.


Vocalizations
Few vocalizations have been recorded for the band-tailed nighthawk. It has a gole-kwoik-kwak call that is at a similar pitch to a frog's ribbit, and is repeated steadily and at a low, subtle volume; it is considered a territorial call.


Conservation and management
The IUCN Red List lists the status of the band-tailed nighthawk as of Least Concern. While Amazonian deforestation is predicted to cause a decrease in its population, it has a large geographic range and is a fairly common species. 


References

The '''band-tailed nighthawk''' ('''''Nyctiprogne leucopyga''''') is a species of [[nightjar]] in the family [[Caprimulgidae]]. It is widely spread throughout northern and central [[South America]]. Five [[subspecies]] are recognised. They are [[Nocturnality|nocturnal]] birds that feed on flying insects. As with almost all nightjars, they lay their eggs directly on the ground and rely their camouflaged plumage to avoid predation.

== Taxonomy ==

The band-tailed nighthawk was [[species description|formally described]] and illustrated in 1825 by the German naturalist [[Johann Baptist von Spix]] under the [[binomial name]] ''Caprimulgus leucopygus''. Spix specified the habitat as the wooded shores of the Amazon.<ref>{{ cite book | last=von Spix | first=Johann Baptist | author-link=Johann Baptist von Spix | year=1825 | title=Avium species novae, quas Brasiliam anus MDCCCXVII - MDCCCXX | volume=2 | language=Latin | location=Monachii [Munich] | publisher=Franc. Seraph. Hübschmanni | page=3; Plate 3, fig. 2 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/41277958 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Peters | editor-first=James Lee | editor-link=James L. Peters | year=1940 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=4 | publisher=Harvard University Press | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts  | page=189 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14476660 }}</ref> The specific epithet is derived from [[Ancient Greek]] λευκοπυγος/''leukopugos'' meaning "with white rear", from λευκος/''leukos'' meaning "white" and -πυγος/''-pugos'' meaning "-rumped".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=leucopygus | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=leucopygus | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=3 July 2025}}</ref> The band-tailed nighthawk is now placed together with the [[plain-tailed nighthawk]] in the genus ''[[Nyctiprogne]]'' that was introduced in 1847 by the French naturalist [[Charles Lucien Bonaparte]].<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=February 2025 | title=Frogmouths, Oilbird, potoos, nightjars | work=IOC World Bird List Version 15.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/nightjars/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=3 July 2025 }}</ref>

=== Subspecies ===
There are 5 recognized [[subspecies]] which differ in size, colour and vocalizations.<ref name=ioc/><ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=Yoon |first=H. S. |date=2020 |editor-last=Schulenberg |editor-first=T. S. |title=Band-tailed Nighthawk (Nyctiprogne leucopyga) |url=https://doi-org.proxy3.library.mcgill.ca/10.2173/bow.batnig1.01 |website=Birds of the World |publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=Cleere |first=N. |date=2010 |title=Nightjars, Potoos, Frogmouths, Oilbird, and Owlet-nightjars of the World |url=https://doi-org.proxy3.library.mcgill.ca/10.1515/9781400836161 |journal=Princeton: Princeton University Press |volume=9 |page=104}}</ref> The subspecies ''N. l. latifascia'' has been suggested to be a separate species.<ref name=":10" /> It is visually distinct from the other subspecies, as its plumage is darker in colour, and there are differences in its vocalizations.<ref name=":10">{{Cite journal |last=Sigurðsson |first=Snorri |last2=Cracraft |first2=Joel |date=2014 |title=Deciphering the diversity and history of New World nightjars (Aves: Caprimulgidae) using molecular phylogenetics: Phylogeny of New World Nightjars |url=http://doi.wiley.com/10.1111/zoj.12109 |journal=Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society |language=en |volume=170 |issue=3 |pages=506–545 |doi=10.1111/zoj.12109|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

* ''N. l. exigua'' [[Herbert Friedmann|Friedmann]], 1945 – east Colombia and south Venezuela.  Smaller and has darker that the nominate.
* ''N. l. pallida'' [[William H. Phelps Sr.|Phelps, WH]] & [[William H. Phelps Jr.|Phelps, WH Jr]], 1952 – northeast Colombia and west, central Venezuela. Paler upperparts than the nominate.
* ''N. l. leucopyga'' ([[Johann Baptist von Spix|Spix]], 1825) – east Venezuela, the Guianas and north Brazil
* ''N. l. latifascia'' Friedmann, 1945 – extreme south Venezuela
* ''N. l. majuscula'' [[Olivério Pinto|Pinto]] & [[Hélio Ferraz de Almeida Camargo|Camargo]], 1952 – northeast Peru, north, east Bolivia and west, central Brazil. Largest of the subspecies, paler with white underparts.

== Description ==
The band-tailed nighthawks is a small species, with a body length of 16.5 to 19 cm (6.5 to 7.5 in),<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":3" /> and a [[wingspan]] of 12.9 to 14.1 cm (5.1 to 5.6 in).<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":4">{{Cite book |last=Cleere |first=Nigel |title=Nightjars: a guide to the nightjars, nighthawks, and their relatives |date=1998 |publisher=Yale University Press |isbn=978-0-300-07457-4 |location=New Haven}}</ref> Adults weigh from 0.023 to 0.028 kg (0.051 to 0.062 lb). The female has a larger average weight and wingspan than the male.

The [[Beak|bill]] is very wide but short, with a length of 0.9 to 0.97 cm (0.35 to 0.38 in), and is hooked at the tip.<ref name=":4" /> It has [[Cryptic coloration|cryptically]] patterned brown [[plumage]], used to blend in with tree bark and leaves. Its wings are long with pointed wingtips, and have no white markings.<ref name=":5">{{Cite journal |last=Costa |first=T. V. V. |last2=Van Els |first2=P. |last3=Braun |first3=M. J. |last4=Whitney |first4=B. M. |last5=Cleere |first5=N. |last6=Sigurðsson |first6=S. |last7=Silveira |first7=L. F. |date=2023 |title=Systematic revision and generic classification of a clade of New World nightjars (Caprimulgidae), with descriptions of new genera from South America |journal=Avian Systematics |volume=1 |issue=6 |pages=55-99}}</ref> It has a black bill, dark brown irises, and pinkish-gray [[Tarsus (skeleton)|tarsus]] and feet. The tarsus lengths are generally short, from 1.18 to 1.24 cm (0.46 to 0.49 in).<ref name=":4" /> Unlike many nightjar species, band-tailed nighthawks lack elongated [[Rictal bristle|rictal bristles]].<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":5" /><ref name=":11">{{Cite journal |last=Han |first=Kin-Lan |last2=Robbins |first2=Mark B. |last3=Braun |first3=Michael J. |date=2010-05-01 |title=A multi-gene estimate of phylogeny in the nightjars and nighthawks (Caprimulgidae) |url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S1055790310000254 |journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution |volume=55 |issue=2 |pages=443–453 |doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2010.01.023 |issn=1055-7903|url-access=subscription }}</ref> It has white patches on either side of the throat, and a white band on the mid-tail, across its three outermost tail feathers. The tail itself is short, and somewhat forked, at 8.2 to 9.7 cm (3.2 to 3.8 in).

It has very weak [[sexual dimorphism]], with both sexes presenting similar plumage and colouring.<ref name=":5" /> The immature and juvenile phases are also similar to the mature adult, although are slightly more plain and paler.

== Distribution and habitat ==
The band-tailed nighthawk has a large range, although there are gaps in its distribution.<ref name=":8">{{Cite report |url=http://www.iucnredlist.org/details/22689720/0 |title=Nyctiprogne leucopyga: BirdLife International: The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2016: e.T22689720A93245073 |last=IUCN |date=2016-10-01 |publisher=International Union for Conservation of Nature |doi=10.2305/iucn.uk.2016-3.rlts.t22689720a93245073.en |language=en}}</ref> It is located through much of the northern parts of South America, from east of the [[Andes]] to Venezuela and central Brazil.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":7">{{Cite journal |last=Milensky |first=Christopher M. |last2=Hinds |first2=Wiltshire |last3=Aleixo |first3=Alexandre |last4=Maria de Fátima |first4=C. Lima |date=2005 |title=BIRDS |url=http://www.bioone.org/doi/abs/10.2988/0097-0298%282005%2913%5B43%3AB%5D2.0.CO%3B2 |journal=Bulletin of the Biological Society of Washington |language=en |volume=13 |issue=1 |pages=43–74 |doi=10.2988/0097-0298(2005)13[43:B]2.0.CO;2 |issn=0097-0298|url-access=subscription }}</ref> It is found from northern Venezuela and eastern Colombia, east to [[French Guiana]] and eastern Brazil. Its range then extends south to northern [[Bolivia]] and northeastern Paraguay.<ref name=":1" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=Nyctiprogne leucopyga (Band-tailed Nighthawk) - Avibase |url=https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?lang=EN&avibaseid=73F8FC019D68C196&sec=summary |access-date=2024-10-15 |website=avibase.bsc-eoc.org}}</ref> It is also found in Peru, [[Ecuador]], and [[Guyana]].<ref name=":7" />

Its natural [[habitat]] is in [[Subtropics|subtropical]] and [[Tropics|tropical]] areas located near water, along [[River|rivers]], [[Stream|streams]], [[Marsh|marshes]], and [[Lake|lakes]].<ref name=":1" /> These habitats include [[Upland and lowland|lowland]] [[Forest|forests]], [[Savanna|savannahs]], [[Grassland|grasslands]], and [[Swamp|swamps]]. It is only reported in areas of elevation below 200 to 300m.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last=Hilty |first=S. L. |title=Birds of Venezuela |date=2003 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-1-4008-3409-9 |edition=2nd |location=Princeton, New Jersey}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite book |last=Hilty |first=Steven L. |title=A guide to the birds of Colombia |last2=Brown |first2=William L. |last3=Tudor |first3=Guy |date=1986 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-0-691-08372-8 |series=Princeton paperbacks |location=Princeton, NJ}}</ref>

== Behaviour and ecology ==
The band-tailed nighthawk is a nocturnal species, and is the most active during the late evening, early morning, and at night. During the day, it [[Roost site|roosts]] near the ground in locations with dense [[Thicket|thickets]] of bushes and trees.<ref name=":5" /> The ecology and behaviour of this nighthawk is not well known, and there have been few dedicated studies<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":6">{{Cite journal |last=Pérez-Granados |first=Cristian |last2=Schuchmann |first2=Karl-L. |date=2022-12-01 |title=Automated signal recognition as a useful tool for monitoring little-studied species: The case of the Band-tailed Nighthawk |url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S1574954122003119 |journal=Ecological Informatics |volume=72 |article-number=101861 |doi=10.1016/j.ecoinf.2022.101861 |issn=1574-9541|hdl=10045/128491 |hdl-access=free }}</ref><ref name=":4" />

=== Breeding ===
Seasonal changes in the vocal activity of the band-tailed nighthawk has been used to determine the timing of its breeding season.<ref name=":6" /> The breeding season begins between July and August, when the maximum singing activity occurs, and the [[dry season]] ends. The [[Nesting season|nesting]] period occurs between September and October, when the [[wet season]] begins and there is a high amount of food availability. There is also less vocal activity measured during this nesting period.

The [[Bird nest|nest]] is a shallow hole on the ground, usually in open areas with little coverage, such as gravel beaches or forest floors.<ref name=":0" /> The nest is lined with leaves and other vegetation. The eggs are cream or white, with small dark blotches.<ref name=":1" />

=== Feeding ===
The band-tailed nighthawk is an insectivore that feeds primarily on aerial [[Arthropod|arthropods]], using a feeding method known as [[Hawking (birds)|hawking]]<ref name=":11" />'''.''' Its diet includes [[Beetle|beetles]], [[Ant|ants]], [[Hemiptera|true bugs]], and flying [[Termite|termites]],<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Alonso |first=José Alvarez |last2=Whitney |first2=Bret M. |date=2003-08-01 |title=New Distributional Records of Birds From White-Sand Forests of the Northern Peruvian Amazon, With Implications for Biogeography of Northern South America |url=https://academic.oup.com/condor/article/105/3/552/5563354 |journal=The Condor |volume=105 |issue=3 |pages=552–566 |doi=10.1093/condor/105.3.552 |issn=0010-5422}}</ref> amongst other arthropods. It forages in continuous flight, with rapid, shallow wingbeats and gliding to keep it aloft.<ref name=":5" /> It feeds mostly at [[dusk]], when there are high amounts of flying insects.

Feeding occurs around sunset and stops once it becomes too dark for the bird to see its prey, and then occurs once more just before dawn.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":5" /> In between feeding sessions, it roosts in the same area, on thin horizontal branches close to vegetation and water. The band-tailed nighthawk roosts perpendicular to the branch, either by itself or in groups of 2-10 individuals.<ref name=":5" />

=== Vocalizations ===
Few [[Bird vocalization|vocalizations]] have been recorded for the band-tailed nighthawk.<ref name=":6" /> It has a gole-kwoik-kwak call that is at a similar pitch to a frog's ribbit, and is repeated steadily and at a low, subtle volume;<ref name=":6" /> it is considered a territorial call.

== Conservation and management ==
The IUCN Red List lists the status of the band-tailed nighthawk as of Least Concern.<ref name=":8" /> While Amazonian deforestation is predicted to cause a decrease in its population, it has a large geographic range and is a fairly common species.<ref name=":0" />
